meta.title: Nonlinear Systemics — The Science Between the Sciences
meta.description: An applied complex systems science practice. Research, consulting, and publishing across health, learning, and civilizational systems.

hero.statement: Our most difficult problems often are embedded in systemic complexities that do not respond well to traditional linear solutions. This is because they contain non-linear systemic factors. We specialize in finding those factors and their solutions.

identity: Complex systems at every scale fail in recognizable ways, from chronic illness to organizational dysfunction to civilizational drift. The failure patterns are predictable when the complex attractors are mapped. Attractors are: stable configurations maintained by feedback dynamics. Nonlinear Systemics develops and applies the science of diagnosing those attractors and shifting them.

publications.featured_excerpt: In 1963, a group of mid-century intellectuals asked a question that turned out to be premature by sixty years. Raymond Aron, Robert Oppenheimer, and George Kennan were not asking whether technology was good or bad — they were asking what technology does to the social order. They were right then. The diagnosis has been correct for sixty years. The response still hasn't arrived at the necessary scale.

research.section_intro: Five active research programs, one methodology — attractor engineering — applied across every scale of complex systems. Each program is a distinct application domain; all share the same complex systems framework. The foundational methods are documented in the Systems Science Update.

about.name: Kurt Rowley, PhD
about.heading: About the Practice
paragraphs: Nonlinear Systemics is an applied complex systems science practice. We conduct original applied research, consult with clients, and publish important generalizable findings. Our work focuses on understanding and diagnosing complex nonlinear problems, then designing and testing interventions: biological, social, and technological.

Our core claim is that the most important problems of this century are attractor problems. These are situations where a harmful complex systemic pattern has become stable, self-maintaining, and not susceptible to typical technical or compliance solutions. These are similar to the mathematical concept of strange attractors. Complex problems often require attractor engineering: identifying what holds the patterns in place, finding the leverage points holding attractors in place, and designing interventions that shift the attractor patterns.

The science for doing this exists but is highly interdisciplinary and generally not organized at the levels required to tackle the hardest systemic challenges. We apply expert-level complex systems engineering methods at the appropriate scale for the problems of focus in our research and consulting practice.
